a: Foundry is a cloud platform where an organization brings all its data into one place, cleans and connects it, and builds apps and analysis on top of it. The big idea is that data, the logic that shapes it, and the apps that use it all live together in one system.
fs: Foundry is an integrated data platform: it pulls source data in, transforms it into pipelines, and turns the results into a shared model teams build apps on.
fj: Thinking Foundry is just a database or a BI dashboard tool; it spans the whole flow from raw data to pipelines to operational apps.

q: What is a dataset in Foundry?
a: A dataset is the basic unit of data in Foundry, much like a table with rows and columns. Under the hood it is really a folder of files that Foundry tracks for you, and it keeps a history of every version so you can look back at older states.
fs: A dataset is Foundry's core data object: a versioned, file-backed table that pipelines read as inputs and write as outputs.
fj: Thinking a dataset is a single static file; it is versioned and is usually produced or updated by a pipeline.

q: What is a transform in Foundry?
a: A transform is one step of logic that reads input datasets and produces an output dataset, like 'clean this data' or 'join these two tables'. Chaining many transforms together forms a data pipeline that turns raw data into clean, ready-to-use data.
fs: A transform is one input-to-output step; stringing transforms together builds the pipeline from raw source data to finished tables.
fj: Thinking a transform edits the input dataset in place; it leaves inputs untouched and writes a brand new output dataset.

q: What is the Ontology in Foundry?
a: The Ontology is a shared map that turns your cleaned datasets into real-world concepts like Customer, Order, or Flight. Instead of thinking in tables and columns, people and apps work with these meaningful objects and the links between them.
fs: The Ontology is a semantic layer over your datasets: it names the business concepts, their properties, and relationships so apps speak in objects, not raw rows.
fj: Thinking the Ontology is another database storing a copy of the data; it is a meaning layer mapped on top of existing datasets.

q: What is an object type in the Ontology?
a: An object type is the definition of one kind of real-world thing, like 'Customer', describing what fields it has. A single object is then one specific instance of it, like one particular customer, and usually each row of a dataset becomes one object.
fs: An object type is the schema for a concept (its properties); an object is one instance, much like a table defines columns and a row is one record.
fj: Mixing up the object type (the kind, like Customer) with a single object (one specific customer).
